title: Shopping
shortSummary: Buy things, ask prices, bargain politely, and pay in Hindi markets and shops.
culturalNote: Indian shopping spans tiny street stalls, weekly markets, and air-conditioned malls, and the rules differ. In local markets and with street vendors, polite bargaining (मोल-भाव) is expected and even enjoyed; a friendly 'kuch kam kijiye' (please reduce it a little) is normal, but never aggressive. In fixed-price shops and malls, prices are set and bargaining is out of place. Cash is still common, but digital payment apps using UPI (you may hear 'QR code scan kijiye') are everywhere, even with small vendors. The currency is the rupaya (रुपया), and prices are often quoted per kilo for food. When a shopkeeper says 'lijiye' (please take it) while handing something over, a simple dhanyavaad is enough.

grammar.rule: Ka, ke, ki agreement in prices
grammar.explanation: The price-link word ka (का) changes to ki (की) before feminine nouns and ke (के) before plurals. Yah kitne ka hai uses masculine ka; for a feminine item like a book you would say yah kitne ki hai.

examples.native: यह शर्ट कितने की है? (yah shirt kitne ki hai?)
ये जूते कितने के हैं? (ye joote kitne ke hain?)

grammar.commonMistake: Using ka with every noun; the form must agree with gender and number.

grammar.explanation: To ask if someone has something, Hindi uses ke paas (literally 'near'): aapke paas chai hai? means 'do you have tea?'. There is no separate verb 'to have'; possession is expressed with paas plus hai.

examples.native: आपके पास छाता है? (aapke paas chhaata hai?)
मेरे पास पैसे हैं। (mere paas paise hain.)

grammar.commonMistake: Translating 'have' with a single verb; Hindi needs the paas construction.

dialogue.setup: Priya shops for a scarf at a market stall run by a vendor, Imran.

lines.speaker: Priya
lines.native: नमस्ते! यह स्कार्फ़ कितने का है?
lines.transliteration: namaste! yah scarf kitne ka hai?
lines.english: Hello! How much is this scarf?

lines.speaker: Imran
lines.native: नमस्ते! तीन सौ रुपये का है।
lines.transliteration: namaste! teen sau rupaye ka hai.

lines.speaker: Priya
lines.native: बहुत महँगा है। कुछ कम कीजिए।
lines.transliteration: bahut mehanga hai. kuch kam kijiye.
lines.english: That's very expensive. Please reduce it a little.

lines.speaker: Imran
lines.native: ठीक है, दो सौ पचास दे दीजिए।
lines.transliteration: theek hai, do sau pachaas de dijiye.
lines.english: Okay, give two hundred and fifty.

lines.speaker: Priya
lines.native: क्या आपके पास नीला रंग है?
lines.transliteration: kya aapke paas neela rang hai?
lines.english: Do you have it in blue?

lines.speaker: Priya
lines.native: ठीक है, मैं कार्ड से दूँगी। रसीद दीजिए।
lines.transliteration: theek hai, main card se doongi. raseed dijiye.
lines.english: Okay, I'll pay by card. Please give a receipt.

quiz.question: How do you ask 'How much is this?' in Hindi?
quiz.correctAnswer: यह कितने का है? (yah kitne ka hai?)
quiz.explanation: Kitne ka hai asks the price of a masculine item.

quiz.question: Which phrase politely asks for a lower price?
quiz.explanation: Kuch kam kijiye means 'please reduce it a little'.

quiz.question: आपके _____ छाता है? (Do you have an umbrella?)
quiz.explanation: Ke paas plus hai is how Hindi expresses 'to have'.

quiz.question: What does 'मैं सिर्फ़ देख रहा हूँ' mean?
quiz.explanation: Sirf means 'only/just' and dekh raha hoon is 'am looking'.

quiz.question: Arrange: चाहिए / यह / मुझे (I want this)
quiz.explanation: Mujhe (to me), then the object yah, then chahiye at the end.
